Possession of these titles determines regulatory control, especially over commercial fishing activities. The issue has gained importance following the National Park Service's decision to phase out commercial fishing in Glacier Bay in October 1999. This disagreement directly affects the 33 coastal communities and numerous tiny settlements in the region.
Summary: Alaska and the federal government disputed the ownership and control of submerged lands and marine waters in Southeast Alaska.

Explanation: Alaskan fishers are mobilizing against USA_e005 (a halt to all groundfish fishing within 20 miles of Steller sea lion rookeries and haul-outs). The restricted areas include three huge ocean zones and 20-mile rings, more than 120 of them, around sea lion rookeries and haulouts from Prince William Sound to Kodiak to the western Aleutians. Fishers argue that this policy has restricted the US' largest commercial fishery, hurting fishers, processing plants, and  small Alaskan communities. Fishers are pressuring Congressional intervention and protesting at docks. The reason for the Steller sea lion population decline is believed to be a combination of a warming ocean and highly efficient industrial fishing,
Summary: Alaskan fishers mobilizedagainst USA_e005 (a halt to all groundfish fishing within 20 miles of Steller sea lion rookeries and haul-outs).

Actor2: South Korean Trawler Kum Kang San
Explanation: The South Korean factory trawler Kum Kang San was seized and taken to Dutch Harbor after deploying its nets inside the US EEZ. Foreign factory trawlers crowd the international boundary because of overfishing in Russia.
Summary: The South Korean factory trawler Kum Kang San was seized and taken to Dutch Harbor after deploying its nets inside the US EEZ.

Location: Bering Sea, 400 yards inside U.S. economic zone waters
Explanation: A Russian fishing boat, Ekarma-3, was seized after being caught illegally fishing within U.S. waters in the Bering Sea. The U.S. Coast Guard Cutter Jarvis, in collaboration with the Russian Federal Border Service, captured the vessel and escorted it to Petropavlovsk, Russia.
Summary: A Russian fishing boat, Ekarma-3, was seized after being caught illegally fishing within U.S. waters in the Bering Sea.

Location: Bering Sea, 800 yards inside U.S. Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ)
Actor1: Russian trawler Spitak and its 20 person crew
Explanation: A Russian trawler, Spitak, was seized by the U.S. Coast Guard for fishing inside the U.S. Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) in the Bering Sea. The event involved the trawler's crew attempting to flee into Russian waters before being apprehended. The trawler was found with approximately 150 pounds of fish onboard and was escorted to Dutch Harbor for further processing.
Summary: A Russian trawler, Spitak, was seized by the U.S. Coast Guard for fishing inside the U.S. Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) in the Bering Sea.

Explanation: Chinese factory trawler Ming Change was seized by the US Coast Guard and Russian Patrol. The vessel was taken to Dutch harbor. Foreign factory trawlers crowd the international boundary because of overfishing in Russia.
Summary: Chinese factory trawler Ming Change was seized by the US Coast Guard and Russian Patrol.

Location: Bering Sea, over 600 yards inside the U.S.-Russia maritime boundary
Explanation: A South Korean trawler was seized by the U.S. Coast Guard for illegally fishing inside U.S. waters in the Bering Sea. The trawler, Orchid, was detected by a Coast Guard plane and pursued until a Coast Guard cutter and Russian Federal Border Service officers detained it. The trawler was carrying 15 metric tons of pollock and Pacific cod and was escorted to Dutch Harbor. Foreign factory trawlers crowd the international boundary because of overfishing in Russia.
Summary: A South Korean trawler, Orchid, was seized by the U.S. Coast Guard for illegally fishing inside U.S. waters in the Bering Sea.

Actor1: Arctic Wind, a Korean-owned, Russian crewed and Honduran-flagged driftnetter
Explanation: The 177-foot Arctic Wind, a Korean-owned, Russian crewed and Honduran-flagged driftnetter, was seized in May after it was spotted south of the Aleutians. About 1.5 tons of salmon was found aboard the vessel. High-seas driftnetting is illegal under international treaty.
Summary: The 177-foot Arctic Wind, a Korean-owned, Russian crewed and Honduran-flagged driftnetter, was seized in May after it was spotted south of the Aleutians.

event_id: alaska1528, alaska9175, arctic6342, arctic15738, alaska9239, alaska12045
Location: Near coasts from Cordova to Attu
Actor1: U.S. District Court Judge Thomas Zilly of Seattle
Explanation: U.S. District Court Judge Thomas Zilly of Seattle ordered a halt to all groundfish fishing within 20 miles of Steller sea lion rookeries and haul-outs. Zilly said the National Marine Fisheries Service had not proved its regulations would protect the declining population of Steller sea lions in the western Gulf and the Aleutian Islands. The judge's order effectively closes most of the near-coast areas to groundfish harvests from Cordova to Attu. Industry spokesmen have said the ban will hit small fishermen the hardest because they don't have boats capable of venturing safely 20 miles into the open ocean. Reports claim that sea lions are declining because of a shift in the ocean environment and the rise of highly efficient industrial fishing.
Summary: U.S. District Court Judge Thomas Zilly of Seattle ordered a halt to all groundfish fishing within 20 miles of Steller sea lion rookeries and haul-outs.

Explanation: A Russian fishing vessel named Gemini was found fishing illegally within U.S. waters by more than half a mile. The U.S. Coast Guard and members of the Russian Federal Border Service boarded the vessel. Russian officials subsequently seized the vessel and escorted it back to Petropavlovsk on Russia's Kamchatka Peninsula.
Summary: A Russian fishing vessel named Gemini was found fishing illegally within U.S. waters by more than half a mile.

Location: Maritime boundary line between Russia and Alaska in the Bering Sea, inside Russian MBL
Actor2: Russian Federal Security Service (FSB) Coast Guard
Explanation: The US Coast Guard spotted Russian Trawler Kapitan Maslovets fishing approximately 1,000 yards across the Russia-USA MBL. When it was seen, the vessel bolted west and was seized in Russian waters by the Russian Federal Border Service
Summary: The US Coast Guard spotted Russian Trawler Kapitan Maslovets fishing approximately 1,000 yards across the Russia-USA MBL.

Explanation: A fisheries dispute event occurred when a Russian delegation walked out of a joint Russian-Norwegian fishing committee meeting in protest to the Norwegian coast guard arrested the Russian trawler Chernigov (NOR_e001). The Russian delegation delivered a protest not to Norwegian diplomats and stated that they would rejoin the talks once the dispute was resolved.
Summary: A Russian delegation walked out of a joint Russian-Norwegian fishing committee meeting in protest to the Norwegian coast guard arresting a Russian trawler.

Location: Bering Sea, 360 miles northwest of St. Paul Island
Explanation: The U.S. Coast Guard seized a Korean trawler, Man Jeok, suspected of illegally fishing in U.S. waters. The vessel had 1,300 tons of pollock aboard/
Summary: The U.S. Coast Guard seized a Korean trawler, Man Jeok, suspected of illegally fishing in U.S. waters.

Location: Bering Sea, U.S. waters seven miles inside MBL
Explanation: A Russian trawler named Petropavlovsk was seized by the U.S. Coast Guard in the Bering Sea for illegal fishing within U.S. waters off Alaska. The vessel was using trawl nets seven miles inside U.S. waters. Russian Federal Board Service officers assisted in boarding the vessel, which was experiencing engine problems. The trawler was being towed to Dutch Harbor for inspection.
Summary: A Russian trawler named Petropavlovsk was seized by the U.S. Coast Guard in the Bering Sea for illegal fishing within U.S. waters off Alaska.

Location: Around Spitsbergen, Svalbard archipelago, Barents Sea
Explanation: A fisheries dispute event occurred when the Norwegian coast guard arrested the Russian trawler Chernigov near Spitsbergen for using a net with too fine a mesh, which they claimed violated fishing agreements.
Summary: The Norwegian coast guard arrested the Russian trawler Chernigov near Spitsbergen.

Explanation: Russia and Norway have a dispute over fishing rights in the Arctic Spizberg archipelago. The conflict has involved navies from both countries. In June, Russia sent a cruiser to the region to protect its fishing boats.
Summary: Russia and Norway dispute fishing rights in the Arctic Spizberg archipelago - Russia sent a cruiser to the region to protect its fishing boats.

Location: Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area
Actor2: Catcher processor vessels and catcher vessels 60 feet and longer using pot gear
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited directed fishing for Pacific cod in the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands. The restrictions affect catcher processor vessels using pot gear and catcher vessels 60 feet in length or longer, and are to prevent exceeding the A season apportionment of the 2002 Pacific cod total allowable catch allocated.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited directed fishing for Pacific cod in the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands.

Location: Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area
Actor2: Yellowfin Sole Fishing vessels using trawl gear
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited the directed fishing for yellowfin sole by vessels using trawl gear in the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area from June 15 to June 30 to prevent exceeding the seasonal halibut bycatch allowance.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited the directed fishing for yellowfin sole by vessels using trawl gear in the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands.

Location: Bycatch Limitation Zone 1 of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands
Actor2: Vessels using trawl gear for yellowfin sole
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has closed directed fishing for yellowfin sole by vessels using trawl gear in Bycatch Limitation Zone 1 of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands. The closure is meant to prevent exceeding the 2002 bycatch allowance of red king crab specified for the trawl yellowfin sole fishery.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has closed directed fishing for yellowfin sole by vessels using trawl gear in Bycatch Limitation Zone 1.

Actor2: Commercial fishing clients (including specific commercial fishermen losing boats, permits, and quota shares)
Explanation: A Homer fishing permit broker, Debbie Moore, defrauded commercial fishing clients of $335,000, subsequently losing it in a Nigerian investment scam. She was sentenced to four years in prison and 10 years of probation, impacting the livelihoods of fishermen reliant on her services, including some who lost their boats, permits, and quota shares.
Summary: A Homer fishing permit broker defrauded commercial fishing clients of $335,000.

Explanation: In December, Norway ended use of the longer boats in a bid to reduce overfishing in response to the European Union decision to decrease the allowable North Sea cod catch. Cod is the backbone of Norwegian wild fish exports
Summary: Norway ended use of the longer boats in a bid to reduce overfishing of North Sea cod catch.

Location: Akutan processing plant, Akutan Island, Alaska
Explanation: Trident Seafoods Corp. was fined $1 million by Alaska officials for air pollution violations at its Akutan processing plant in the Aleutian Islands - the largest seafood production complex in North America. Violations included excessive emissions of nitrogen oxide, using fuel with higher sulfur content than allowed, and failure to maintain adequate records, among other infractions. The company settled the violations by agreeing to pay cash, reduce penalties through environmental fixes, and install advanced technology to lower nitrogen oxide emissions.
Summary: Trident Seafoods Corp. was fined $1 million by Alaska officials for air pollution violations.

Location: Harbors along the Arctic Coast, specifically Lofoten Islands
Explanation: Several Norwegian fishing boats are set to block harbors along Norway's Arctic coast in protest against a government ruling banning trawlers over 28 meters in length to reduce overfishing (NOR_e003). The local fishermen, particularly from the Lofoten Islands, are affected by this ban as they rely heavily on cod fishing in these areas. The blockade also aims to impact the tourist ferry 'Hurtigruta' to draw governmental attention.
Summary: Norwegian fishing boats blocked harbors along Norway's Arctic coast in protest against banning trawlers over 28 meters.

Actor1: U.S. fishing vessels (Arctic Wind, Pacific Star, Ocean Olympic, and Adventure)
Explanation: Four U.S. fishing vessels (the Arctic Wind, Pacific Star, Ocean Olympic, and Adventure) were detected by the Coast Guard fishing for opilio crab in Russian waters, which is a violation of federal law. The Coast Guard directed these vessels to cease their operations and return to port for further investigation by federal and state authorities. The incident marks the first known occurrence of U.S. vessels fishing in Russian waters. The vessels are being monitored by the Coast Guard until they dock.
Summary: Four U.S. fishing vessels  were detected by the Coast Guard fishing for opilio crab in Russian waters.

Actor1: U.S.-based fishing vessels the Alaskan Beauty and Fierce Allegience
Explanation: Two U.S.-based fishing vessels (the Alaskan Beauty and the Fierce Allegiance) were detected by the Coast Guard fishing illegally in Russian waters during the snow crab season, joining four others already under investigation for similar violations (RUS_e003). The National Marine Fisheries Service is seizing the crab loads from these vessels, and the proceeds from the sales will be held until investigations are complete. This enforcement action is under the Lacey Act, which prohibits the acquisition of fish taken in violation of U.S. treaties or foreign laws.
Summary: Two U.S.-based fishing vessels were detected by the Coast Guard fishing illegally in Russian waters during the snow crab season.

Explanation: Icicle Seafoods, a Seattle-based company, was fined $3.4 million for allegedly using two smaller companies to exceed its quota for Alaskan brown king crab. The company is accused of using Adak Fisheries and Adak Fisheries Development as shell corporations to exceed their crab catch quota, thus dominating the market unfairly. This allowed them to catch and process more than 4.4 million pounds of brown king crab, far beyond the allotted 221,000 pounds, negatively impacting other competitors in the market. The dispute centers around whether Icicle acted in violation of the American Fisheries Act, which intends to limit the amount of catch to ensure fair competition and prevent large companies from monopolizing the industry.
Summary: Icicle Seafoods was fined $3.4 million for allegedly using two smaller companies to exceed its quota for Alaskan brown king crab.

Explanation: Fishers in Western Alaska strongly protest the state Board of Fisheries tripling the time seine and gillnet fishermen can catch sockeye salmon around False Pass in June, lifting restrictions that had been in place the last three years. Western fishers say incidental catch of chum salmon will cut off their dwindling subsistence supply, as well as subsistence sockeye in some areas of Bristol Bay.
Summary: Fishers in Western Alaska strongly protest the state Board of Fisheries tripling the time seine and gillnet fishermen can catch sockeye salmon.

event_id: arctic6809, arctic6861, arctic6865, arctic6879, arctic6844, alaska12316, alaska12364
Location: Bering Sea near the wreckage of the Selendang Ayu, from Spray Cape to Cape Kovrizhka along the western side of Unalaska Island
Explanation: On December 28, 2004, state regulators banned commercial fishing in the Bering Sea near the wreckage of the Selendang Ayu, a grounded freighter that has been leaking oil since December 8. The ban affects the Makushin Bay and Skan Bay Tanner crab fisheries, as well as Pacific cod, black rockfish, and other groundfish fisheries. The area of the ban stretches from Spray Cape to Cape Kovrizhka on the western side of Unalaska Island. The closure is expected to impact 20-30 fishing vessels. The freighter, managed by IMC Shipping and flagged from Malaysia, began leaking oil after its crew was unable to restart the engine, leading to a wreck.
Summary: State regulators banned commercial fishing in the Bering Sea near the wreckage of the Selendang Ayu, a grounded freighter that has been leaking oil.

Location: Central Aleutian District, Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area, Alaska
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited directed fishing for Atka mackerel in the Central Aleutian District of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area to prevent exceeding the 2004 total allowable catch.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited directed fishing for Atka mackerel in the Central Aleutian District.

Location: Davis Strait, off Nunavut's Baffin Island coast
Actor2: Federal Department of Fisheries and Oceans (Ottawa)
Explanation: The government of Nunavut is contesting the allocation of northern shrimp quotas by the federal Department of Fisheries and Oceans, which awarded a significant portion of the quotas to southern Canadian fishers instead of local Nunavut fishers. Despite guidelines suggesting Nunavut should receive all new quotas until their allocation matches other provinces, only a portion was awarded. This decision is seen as politically motivated to benefit large fishing companies in Atlantic Canada at the expense of Nunavut's fishery development.
Summary: The government of Nunavut is contesting the allocation of northern shrimp quotas by the federal Department of Fisheries and Oceans.

Location: Glacier Bay and other parts of Southeast Alaska
Explanation: There is a dispute between the U.S. Department of Justice and the State of Alaska over is submerged land in Glacier Bay and smaller pockets in Chatham Strait, Frederick Sound, Stephens Passage and other inland channels belong to Alaska. Ownership will determine who can impose fishing bans, allow cruise ship traffic, and other management policies in these areas.
Summary: There is an ongoing dispute between the U.S. Department of Justice and the State of Alaska over its submerged land.

Location: Svalbard Zone, Arctic seas around Svalbard
Explanation: There is a fisheries dispute between Iceland and Norway over an 80,000-ton annual herring quota in the Svalbard Zone, as per the 1933 Svalbard Treaty. Norway has ordered Iceland to stop herring fishing in the area, while Iceland disputes Norway's right to set the quota and threatens retaliation if the Norwegian Coast Guard interferes with their fishermen.
Summary: There is a fisheries dispute between Iceland and Norway over an 80,000-ton annual herring quota in the Svalbard Zone.

Location: US Military Thule base, north-west of Greenland
Explanation: Greenland Inuit hunters displaced 50 years ago to allow the expansion of a US military base are seeking to return to their original hunting and fishing grounds. They have taken their case to the European Court of Human Rights, accusing the Danish state of being responsible for their forced displacement. The Inuits argue that their displacement has severely impacted their ability to live off the natural resources.
Summary: Greenland Inuit hunters seek to return to their original hunting and fishing grounds.

Explanation: The North Pacific Fishery Management Council voted to ban bottom trawling over a substantial area off Alaska's Aleutian Islands to protect coral beds and other sensitive habitats. This decision covers more than half the fishable waters around the Aleutians and some parts of the northern Gulf of Alaska. The move was in response to a 2000 lawsuit by environmental groups accusing the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration of failing to adequately protect fish habitats. Despite opposition from the commercial fishing industry, the ban is not expected to severely impact the industry since the most lucrative fishing areas remain open.
Summary: The North Pacific Fishery Management Council voted to ban bottom trawling over a substantial area off Alaska's Aleutian Islands to protect coral beds.

Explanation: The Alaska Public Offices Commission fined state Senate President Ben Stevens for not disclosing his chairmanship at the Alaska Fisheries Marketing Board and investigated other allegations related to his financial disclosures and potential conflicts of interest with his options in a fish processing company. He held an option to buy 25 percent of Adak Fisheries, which was the beneficiary of a congressionally approved pollock allocation pushed in 2003 by Stevens' father, U.S. Sen. Ted Stevens, R-Alaska.
Summary: The Alaska Public Offices Commission fined state Senate President Ben Stevens for not disclosing his chairmanship at the Alaska Fisheries Marketing Board.

Location: Approximately 300 miles northwest of Unalaska, near the Bering Sea
Actor2: Alaska State Troopers and Coast Guard
Explanation: The fisheries dispute event involves the Sea Warrior's crew, who were charged with illegally fishing for opilio crab using crab pots belonging to the Big Valley, a vessel that sank on the day the opilio crab fishery was set to begin. The Sea Warrior resumed fishing with the Big Valley's additional 15 crab pots, exceeding the legal limit of gear on board, and were subsequently caught and charged by authorities.
Summary: The Sea Warrior's crew were charged with illegally fishing for opilio crab using crab pots belonging to a vessel that sank.

Location: Arctic waters around the Svalbard Islands
Actor2: Spanish trawlers Monte Meixueiro and Garoya Segundo
Explanation: The Norwegian police arrested Spanish trawlers Monte Meixueiro and Gaoya Segundo for illegal fishing.
Summary: The Norwegian police arrested Spanish trawlers Monte Meixueiro and Gaoya Segundo for illegal fishing.

Location: Arctic waters around the Svalbard Islands
Explanation: There is an ongoing diplomatic dispute between Norway and Spain, as Spain, backed by the European Union, does not accept Norway's claims over the Svalbard Islands' waters. The Norwegian police have ordered a Spanish trawler, Monte Meixueiro (NOR_e008), to post a bond of 13 million kroner for illegal fishing and the Spanish authorities believe that it is up to Spain to investigate and possibly fine its own ships in these waters, which they consider to be international.
Summary: There is an ongoing diplomatic dispute between Norway and Spain, as Spain does not accept Norway's claims over the Svalbard Islands' waters.

Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited directed fishing for Atka mackerel using gears other than jig in the Eastern Aleutian District and the Bering Sea subarea, effective January 20, 2004. This measure aims to prevent exceeding the allowable catch for 2005.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited directed fishing for Atka mackerel using gears other than jig in the Eastern Aleutian District and the Bering Sea subarea.

Location: Bycatch Limitation Zone 1, Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area
Actor2: Vessels using trawl gear for yellowfin sole
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has announced the closure of the yellowfin sole fishery using trawl gear in Bycatch Limitation Zone 1 of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area to prevent exceeding the 2005 bycatch allowance of red king crab.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has announced the closure of the yellowfin sole fishery using trawl gear in Bycatch Limitation Zone 1.

Explanation: Norwegian officials seized two Russian fishing vessels for fishing violations. The ships were released Friday after Norwegian authorities received a bank guarantee for fines imposed on the ships.
Summary: Norwegian officials seized two Russian fishing vessels for fishing violations.

Explanation: Russia has decided to ban imports from four Norwegian salmon fish farms due to reported high levels of lead and cadmium. Norway disputes these claims, citing adherence to stringent EU safety standards.
Summary: Russia has decided to ban imports from four Norwegian salmon fish farms due to reported high levels of lead and cadmium.

event_id: arctic21344, arctic21332, arctic21402, arctic21387, arctic21404, arctic21358, arctic21401, arctic21414, arctic21419
Actor2: Captain Valery Yarantsev of the Russian fishing trawler Elektron
Explanation: The event centered around a Russian trawler, Elektron, which fled the Norwegian Coast Guard in custody of two Norwegian inspectors on board, triggering a criminal investigation against the captain, Valery Yarantsev, for illegal fishing and illegal detention. The incident occurred in the waters around Norway's expanded fisheries management zone near the Svalbard Islands. Norway's claims around Svalbard are not recognized by Russia.
Summary: A Russian trawler, Elektron, fled the Norwegian Coast Guard in custody of two Norwegian inspectors on board.

Explanation: Inuit communities on the east side of Greenland have banned all outside fishing for the shrimp stocks that are growing in the absence of summer ice and have decided to exploit them themselves
Summary: Inuit communities on the east side of Greenland have banned all outside fishing for the shrimp stocks.

Location: The Aleutian Islands Habitat Conservation Area
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) introduced a rule to protect the sea floor in the Aleutian Islands of Alaska from destructive bottom trawling by creating the largest protected marine habitat in the United States. The rule prohibits bottom trawl fishing and the use of mobile bottom-tending fishing gear in designated conservation areas. The rules are even more restrictive in six Aleutian Islands Coral Habitat Protection Areas, where all bottom-contact fishing gear, including longlines, is prohibited.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) prohibited bottom trawl fishing and the use of mobile bottom-tending fishing gear in designated conservation areas.

Location: Mezen district, White Sea, Arctic region, Russia
Explanation: The Russian Government has banned the use of fishing nets by non-professionals. In Mezen, a remote district in Russia's Arctic region by the White Sea, there is significant resentment toward Moscow's fishing regulations. The local population relies heavily on fishing for sustenance and is frustrated by the law, feeling criminalized and marginalized. This discontent is emblematic of the broader dissatisfaction with distant political leaders impinging on local traditions and livelihoods.
Summary: The Russian Government has banned the use of fishing nets by non-professionals.

Location: Aleutian Islands subarea of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area (BSAI)
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service has prohibited directed fishing for Greenland turbot in the Aleutian Islands subarea of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area to prevent exceeding the 2008 total allowable catch. The closure is effective from August 15, 2008, and aims to regulate fishing in accordance with established limits.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service has prohibited directed fishing for Greenland turbot in the Aleutian Islands.

Explanation: The fisheries dispute between Russia and Norway revolves around the contested authority over fishing waters near the Arctic Ocean archipelago of Svalbard. This has escalated with the recent clashes between Russian trawlers and the Norwegian Navy over accusations of illegal fishing. In response, Russia has sent `two ships to protect its fishing fleet, leading to heightened tensions.
Summary: Russia has sent two ships to protect its fishing fleet around the contested Arctic Ocean archipelago of Svalbard., leading to heightened tensions.

Actor2: Vessels participating in the BSAI trawl limited access fishery
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has issued a prohibition on directed fishing for Atka mackerel in the Central Aleutian District of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area by vessels in the BSAI trawl limited access fishery. This measure is to prevent the total allowable catch from being exceeded for 2008.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has issued a prohibition on directed fishing for Atka mackerel in the Central Aleutian District and Aleutian Islands.

Location: Eastern Aleutian District and Bering Sea subarea of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area (BSAI)
Actor2: Vessels participating in the BSAI trawl limited access fishery
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited directed fishing for Atka mackerel in the Eastern Aleutian District and the Bering Sea subarea of the BSAI for vessels participating in the BSAI trawl limited access fishery. This regulatory action is aimed at preventing the total allowable catch for the 2008 A season from being exceeded.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited directed fishing for Atka mackerel in the Eastern Aleutian District and the Bering Sea subarea.

Location: Eastern Aleutian District and Bering Sea subarea of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area (BSAI)
Actor2: Vessels participating in the BSAI trawl limited access fishery
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited directed fishing for Atka mackerel in the Eastern Aleutian District and Bering Sea subarea by vessels participating in the BSAI trawl limited access fishery to prevent exceeding the 2008 total allowable catch.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has prohibited directed fishing for Atka mackerel in the Eastern Aleutian District and Bering Sea subarea.

Location: Eastern Aleutian District and Bering Sea subarea of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area (BSAI), Alaska
Actor2: Vessels participating in the Amendment 80 limited access fishery
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) is closing directed fishing for Atka mackerel for vessels in the Amendment 80 limited access fishery in the Eastern Aleutian District and Bering Sea subarea. This is to prevent exceeding the total allowable catch for the A season of 2008. The regulation change took effect on February 5, 2008.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) is closing directed fishing for Atka mackerel for vessels in the Eastern Aleutian District and Bering Sea subarea.

Actor1: Ron Chambers and his fishing companions
Actor2: Alaskan State Troopers and Coast Guard
Explanation: Ron Chambers, the Yukon Environmental and Socio-economic Assessment Act co-ordinator, along with two fishing companions, were cited for overfishing king salmon and mutilating halibut (cut into small chunks to disguise amount caught) in Alaska. Their infractions were discovered after their boat struck what they thought was a whale, leading to a coast guard response.
Summary: The Yukon Environmental and Socio-economic Assessment Act co-ordinator was cited for overfishing king salmon and mutilating halibut.

Location: Bycatch Limit Zone 1 Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area (BSAI)
Actor2: Vessels participating in the Amendment 80 limited access fishery
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) announced a closure of directed fishing for yellowfin sole by vessels participating in the Amendment 80 limited access fishery in the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands due to the risk of exceeding the seasonal red king crab bycatch allowance.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) announced a closure of directed fishing for yellowfin sole by vessels participating in the Amendment 80 limited access fishery.

Location: Eastern Aleutian District of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands (BSAI) management area
Actor2: Vessels participating in the Amendment 80 limited access fishery
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) is halting directed fishing for Pacific ocean perch in the Eastern Aleutian District of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands (BSAI) management area to prevent exceeding the total allowable catch for 2008. This affects vessels participating in the Amendment 80 limited access fishery.
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) is halting directed fishing for Pacific ocean perch in the Eastern Aleutian District.

Location: Port Gravina, Prince William Sound, Alaska
Actor1: The Nordic Viking, a commercial fishing vessel
Explanation: The Nordic Viking, a commercial fishing vessel, ran aground near Port Gravina in Prince William Sound, resulting in a spill of 3,500 gallons of diesel fuel. This incident prompted state officials to temporarily close certain areas to commercial salmon fishing.
Summary: Sstate officials temporarily closed certain areas to commercial salmon fishing after a vessel ran aground and spilled fuel.

Explanation: The Norwegian coastguard fined the Spanish shipping company Pesquera Ancora NOK 37.6 million (approximately US$ 5.98 million or EUR 4.73 million) after three of its trawlers were caught fishing in the protection zone outside the island of Svalbard, Norway.
Summary: The Norwegian coastguard fined the Spanish shipping company Pesquera Ancora after three of its trawlers were caught fishing in a protection zone.

Location: Alaska, specifically the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands
Actor1: NOAA Fisheries Service Office of Law Enforcement
Actor2: Vessel owners, permit holders, and hired master of the fishing vessel Trident
Explanation: This article discusses penalties and sanctions imposed on individuals associated with the Alaskan fishing vessel Trident for falsely reporting fishing areas. The violations, detected by NOAA's Fisheries Service Office of Law Enforcement, affect the management of Alaska's halibut and sablefish fisheries and unfairly disadvantage those who fish in permitted areas.
Summary: The Alaskan fishing vessel Trident for falsely reporting fishing areas.

Location: Aleutian Islands subarea of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area (BSAI)
Actor2: Fishermen and fishing companies targeting Greenland turbot
Explanation: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has announced a prohibition on directed fishing for Greenland turbot in the Aleutian Islands subarea of the Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands management area. This action, effective May 28, 2009, is necessary to prevent exceeding the 2009 total allowable catch. This closure will ensure compliance with regulations specified under 50 CFR 679.20(d)(1)(iii).
Summary: The National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) has announced a prohibition on directed fishing for Greenland turbot in the Aleutian Islands.

Location: Arctic waters, specifically from the Bering Strait in Alaska to the U.S.-Canada border
Explanation: The U.S. advisory board, North Pacific Fishery Management Council, voted to ban industrial fishing in over 518,000 square kilometers of U.S. Arctic waters. The Kenyan Maritime Authority banned the FV Golden Wave (also known as Geummi 305 or Keummi 305) from fishing in Kenyan waters until it meets the required fishing standards.

Actor2: Allan Githeji (welder), Philip Mutuku (teacher),  David Chege (Task Force leader), Joseph Ndung'u ("hocker")

Later in 2007 all four men were acquitted due to a lack of evidence.

A three month ban has been imposed on Lake ivasha after fishermen allowed on the lake were using illegal nets smaller than regulation size. The nets had affected fish stock growth.

A yearly three month ban is being imposed on Lake ivasha to allow for fish stocks to increase. The ban goes into effect as peak breeding period begins. Pleas were made by the fishermen to allow them to use more nets per boat, but those pleas were rejected.

At Lake ivasha, an illegal fisherman died when he fell into a feeder cal after trying to run from the authorities.

Actor2: Coastal Aquaculture, an Italian-Swiss joint firm

In April, the EU announced minimum standards before the ban could be lifted, including tighter legislation in the control of fishing, proper coordition, and monitoring of fish handling and processes. Some of the causes of the rampant use of poison were allegedly poor policies and weak regulations (ex allowing illegal trawling), industrial pollution, and the increase in pressure on the industry as a result of the expanding number of unemployed who enter the unregulated industry. The East African ministers made a joint appeal to the EU on June 27, asking them to lift the ban and assuring fish imports would be safe for human consumption. EU inspectors announced on Aug 18, 1999, following an inspection of Kenya's fish industry, that they were going to extend the ban on fish imports. Following this announcement, the Range MP accused the Kenyan government for the extension, blaming poor public relations and little action to protect the fishing industry. He claimed they were conspiring with large scale fish processors to impoverish fishermen and their families through stage-maged crises. Stakeholders in the Lake Victoria fishing industry are now accusing the EU of banning their fish after being pressured by stakeholders of the European trawl fishing industry that sees the Nile perch as competition to their White Hake fish. The EU ordered Kenya to reduce the number of landing beaches from the current 150 to nine. Kenyan representatives complained that the EU was not responsive to the samples they sent. The EU wants Kenya and Uganda to harmonize their maritime laws with Tanzanian legislation. The EU delayed lifting Kenya's ban because they failed to improve hygienic conditions. The Kenyan Embassy in Brussels announced the EU decided to lift the ban on Wednesday, November 22, 2000, following a meeting with the EU's standing veteriry committee.

Explanation: The Spanish government announced it would send its warships to Somali waters to guard its fishing vessels against pirates. Spanish vessels illegally fish in Somali waters. The Ministry of Defense in Spain explained the deployment was motivated by the approaching fishing season.

The MV Asmak 1, an Omani fishing vessel, was hijacked near Bosaso. The crew's engineer died in captivity. The vessel was held in Eyl and released 7 months later following efforts by PUntland elders and UAE officials.

Actor1: Deputy Minister of Ports of Puntland Regional Administration, Abdikadir Muse Yusuf
Actor2: Foreign warships, particularly European (Spain and Norway)
Explanation: The Puntland deputy minister of ports, Abdikadir Muse Yusuf, accused powerful countries whose warships are patrolling Somali waters (to help fight the piracy problem) of having relations with pirate groups. He claims the countries are not combating the pirates to the best of their abilities, but instead have special interests. The minister accused some European countries (specifically Spain and Norway) of having warships and using Somali piracy as an excuse to enter Somali waters to harvest fish and other marine resources. He cited Spain's appeal to protect their fishing villages in the region.

The Eritrean government spoke out against what they said was a campaign to cover up looting and gross violation of international laws committed in Somalia's territorial waters. Their statement alleges foreign companies are engaged in looting fisheries and other marine resources and media coverage of pirates seizing ships in Somalia are just rumors trying to cover up illegal fishing and toxic waste dumping. Eritrea also claims foreign governments are using rumors of piracy to increase their military presence in the region. The article indicates that Somali officials will confirm piracy is a real problem and, in the absence of a functioning central authority, pirates justify their actions by claiming they are defenders of the Somali coast.

Actor1: Deputy Speaker of the Somali parliament, Professor Muhammed Umar Dalha
Actor2: Powerful nations using warships to patrol Somali waters for pirates
Explanation: The deputy speaker of the Somali parliament, Professor Muhammad Umar Dalha, accused powerful nations whose warships are in Somali waters to deal with the piracy issue of being complacent with the activities of the Somali pirates, so foreigner countries can take advantage of Somalia's natural resources, while claiming to protect the coast since there is no effective government to secure it. Dahla claims ransom money is intended to support pirates and their efforts to abduct other ships. He says the ransom money could be spent rebuilding the Somali navy, but foreign nations would rather spend it on the pirates he believes they have agreements with.

Location: Off the Kenyan coast near Lamu
Explanation: The Tianyu No. 8, with a crew of 24, was fishing in Kenyan waters near Lamu when Somali pirates hijacked it, claiming they were fishing in Lower Jubba waters. The vessel was then sailed to Kismayo. The pirates accused the vessel of illegal fishing, claiming they found lots of fish and crocodile ears on board. This is another case of pirates accusing foreigners of stealing marine resources.  Piracy became a problem in Somalia without an effective governce mechanism to extinguish it.

Actor1: Al Shabaab spokesman Sheikh Muqtar Ali AKA Abu Mansur
Explanation: Al-Shabaab spokesman Sheikh Muqtar Ali AKA Abu Mansur spoke to a group of residents in Merca. According to Al Shabaab, the foreign ships in Somali waters posing as warships to combat piracy have a hidden agenda to fish the Somali coast - their interests in the region are purely economical. The spokesman added that piracy had been under control under the Islamic Courts, but since being ousted by foreign interference, the new governance structure has not been able to effectively control the problem.

Actor2: A Thai fishing boat, the Ekawat Nava 5
Explanation: The Thai fishing vessel, Ekawatva 5, with a crew of 16, was hijacked by pirates near the Yemeni coast in the Gulf of Aden. The fishing vessel was traveling from from Oman to Yemen to deliver fishing equipment. Soon after getting control of the ship, pirates converted the vessel into a mother ship and fired upon the Indian vy's INS Tabar. The vy responded and sunk the hijacked trawler. One of the hostage fishermen was killed in the assault, 14 were still missing, and one drifted in the ocean for 6 days before being rescued by a passing vessel.

Location: Gulf of Aden, assuming in Yemeni waters because UK vessel intervened eventually
Explanation: Somali pirates hijacked a Yemeni fishing vessel, Waadi Omar 2, and held the crew hostage until they opened fire on two Royal vy assault crafts following an attempted hijack of the Danish vessel the MV Powerful. Two of the pirates were killed in the fire fight. The British vy arrested the 8 pirates and turned them over to the Kenyan court. The spiraling conditions of piracy have been connected to the lack of a central functioning central administration in Somalia.
A Somali fisherman's boat was destroyed when its nets became entangled in those of a foreign fishing trawler illegally fishing in Somali waters. By 2008, that man had become a pirate.

Actor1: Sheik Sharif Sheik Ahmed, modern Islamist Somali opposition leader, representing the Alliance for the Re-liberation of Somalia (ARS)
Explanation: The opposition leader for the Alliance for the Re-liberation of Somalia (ARS) coalition, Sheik Sharif Sheik Ahmed, called upon the international community in Somali waters fighting piracy to put a similar effort into fighting illegal fishing and toxic waste dumping.

Actor2: Two Yemeni fishing boats, the MV Qana'a and the MV Falluja
Explanation: Somali pirates hijacked two Yemeni fishing vessels, the MV Qa'a and the MV Falluja, near the Mait area close to the port of Aden. Seven fishermen maged to escape and report the event. The 22 remaining fishermen were held hostage. The pirates had intended to use the fishing vessels as mother ships for their attacks on other ships in the Gulf of Aden. The MV Falluja was released on Dec 27 and the MV Qa'a was released Jan 2. Following negotiations between pirates and Somali tribal leaders, no ransom was paid for the boats' releases.

Somali pirates hijacked a Yemeni fishing dhow in the Gulf of Aden and used it as a mother ship. The 12 Yemeni hostages were freed when the pirates attempted to hijack a merchant ship (MV Gibe) and the Indian vy (INS Mysore) arrived, arresting all 23 "pirates" on the mother ship. On Dec 17, 2008, Yemen's Defense Ministry announced the arrested Yemeni "pirates" were fishermen held hostage.

Actor2: Chinese fishing vessel, Zhenhua 4; EU Naval Task Force
Explanation: The Chinese fishing vessel the Zhenhua 4, with a crew of 30, was hijacked by pirates. The vessel was subsequently released by the multilateral EU val task force.

Armed pirates boarded a fishing vessel, the Victoria 4, which was at anchor in the Kiunga Harbor. They tied up the crewmembers (4 Kenyans, 3 Indians), stole money and valuable equipment and abducted the 3 Indian sailors, leaving the Kenyan crew members unharmed. The vessel was brought back to Mombasa. The 3 Indian hostages were released on February 23, 2009. Their captures claimed they had been on the Kismaayo coast. There was no information on whether or not a ransom was paid.

The Spanish parliament voted to send up to 365 troops to combat piracy in Somali waters as a part of the European Union Special Task Force. The Defense Minister, Carme Chacon, supported the decision, saying it "is of vital importance for the defense of the geostrategic and economic interests of Spain, and will provide security to [their] fishing fleet." Spain will take command of the task force in 2 months (April 2009) when Spanish fishing boats will be working off the coast of Somalia

Al-Shabaab cut off the hand of a 26-year-old man who was convicted of stealing three sacks full of fishing nets worth $300 from a businesswoman. Al-Shabaab has begun imposing strict Islamic law in areas under their control.
81 Yemenis in 6 fishing boats were arrested by the Somaliland coast guard for fishing illegally in Somaliland waters near Berbera. They were fined and deported back to Yemen a week later.

Actor1: Fishing village in Bayla District, Bari Region; Fishers from Bayla Region
Explanation: FIshermen seized an Iranian commercial fishing boat (likely FV Safari) in the waters of the Bayla District of the Bari region. The boat allegedly was transporting machines and other equipment, but the village fishermen and elders  claim the boat was fishing on the Bayla coast without permission from the Puntland administration. The villagers were motivated to take action after the foreign vessel damaged their own fishing equipment ("spoiling their fishing network") and are holding the ship until the cost of their destroyed boats and nets is paid back.

Location: Somali territorial waters, perhaps near Yemeni port of Mukalla
Explanation: Yemeni fishermen were in Somali territorial waters when they came under fire from Somali pirates, killing one fisherman and injuring two others. The boat managed to escape and return to the Yemeni port of Mukalla.

Actor2: Foreign vessels licensed to fish in Somali waters
Explanation: The Somali National Unity Government revoked fishing licenses for foreign vessels in their waters following complaints by Somali fishermen that foreigners are threatening their livelihoods by forcing locals out. Somali fishermen cannot keep up with foreign vessels because they lack modern equipment and do not have the means to replace old nets. The fisheries minister said it was impossible for the Somali government to monitor their territorial waters, but they were working to develop new legislation to regulate fishing.

A shootout occurred between a private security firm, Som-Can, and two Greek fishing vessels. Som-Can had been hired to patrol the coast by the previous Somali administration of Gen. Adde Muse, who lost the most recent election. Som-Can opened fire on the Greek vessels claiming they did not have proper fishing licenses, though the Greek vessels claimed they had registered to fish in Puntland two weeks prior and were allowed to fish there. The Puntland Ministry of Fisheries has been accused of giving out fishing licenses without proper legal procedure. The coastguard company brought the vessels to Bosaso, where they claim the illegal fishers will be brought to justice, but the Puntland regional administration and regional elders were working to free the ships. The Puntland fishing ministry said the licenses were legitimate, but the private firm had no license to guard the coast. The security company says it was hire to protect the coast and its contract does not end until mid-2009.

Actor1: Dr Abdirahman Mohamed "Farole," President of Puntland
Actor2: Som-Can, Private Coastguard Company; Puntland Ministry of Fisheries
Explanation: The Puntland President, Dr Abdirahman Mohamed "Farole," appointed a committee to investigate the Ministry of Fisheries and a private security firm, Som-Can, that had been hired by the previous Puntland administration of Gen. Adde Muse, who lost the most recent election. Som-Can had recently opened fire on Greek vessels claiming they did not have proper fishing licenses, though the Greek vessels claimed they had registered to fish in Puntland two weeks prior and were allowed to fish there. The Puntland fishing ministry said the licenses were legitimate, but the private firm had no license to guard the coast. The security company says it was hire to protect the coast and its contract does not end until mid-2009. The Puntland Ministry of Fisheries has been accused of giving out fishing licenses without proper legal procedure. The committee is tasked with reviewing licenses issued by the ministry to ensure legality and review the contract between Puntland and Som-Can. The Som-Can personnel are also under review, as many have links to piracy.

Location: Some reports say Gulf of Aden, others say Indian Ocean
Actor2: Yemeni flagged fishing vessel, Al Shahir; Puntland Coast Guard
Explanation: A Yemeni fishing vessel, the Al Shahir, was hijacked by Somali pirates. The 14 person crew became hostages. The Puntland Coast Guard "later" freed the vessel and rescued crew members. Two pirates were arrested and 12 managed to escape.

Actor2: Yemeni fishermen on the Yemeni fishing vessel, Shugaa Almadhi; Yemeni Coast Guard
Explanation: The Yemeni fishing boat, Shugaa Almadhi, was hijacked with a crew of 13 Yemeni men on April 9, 2009. The Yemeni coast guard rescued the vessel in a shootout on April 13, 2009 that resulted in zero casualties. 2 pirates were detained and the other 12 escaped.

Actor2: Egyptian fishermen on two Egyptian fishing vessels, FV Samarah Ahmed and FV Momtaz 1
Explanation: Two Egyptian Fishing Vessels, the FV Samarah Ahmed and the FV Momtaz 1, were hijacked near Ras Kampomi in the vicinity of Bossasso. Both vessels had permits to fish in Somali waters, but their captors insisted they were there illegally and should be brought to justice (Update: there are conflicting reports on whether or not the vessels had licenses). The crew of 33 (total) managed to regain control of their vessels and escape on August 15, 2009. Using machetes and guns, the Egyptian fishers killed two brigands and took 9 pirates to Yemen to turn them over (ultimately the crew decided to sail back to Egypt with the pirates to try them there). One pirate escaped the boat during the uprising. 7 suspected pirates from the Egyptian vessels showed up in the shores of Somalia

Actor1: Coastal communities; Regional leaders in Puntland
Explanation: Regional leaders in Puntland worked together with fishing communities to build a militia to catch pirates following heightened frustrations from coastal fishers whose boats continued to be seized at gunpoint by the bandits. A community managed to capture 12 armed pirates in two boats.

Actor1: Muhammad Abdullahi Umar, foreign minister of the Somali transitional government
Explanation: The foreign minister if the Somali transitional government, Muhammad Abdullahi Umar, accused foreign warships (in Somali waters to combat piracy) of engaging in illegal fishing and dumping the waste products of major foreign companies. Umar claims their actions are responsible for the increase in piracy and the only way to stop foreigners was the formation of a national navy force.

Actor1: Fisheries and Marine Resources Minister Abdirahim Ibbi
Explanation: The Fisheries and Marine Resources Minister for Somalia's interim government held a press conference in Mogadishu to announce that foreign warships were not in Somali waters to fight pirates, but to exploit Somalia's weak governance structure and divide Somalia's marine resources among each other.

Actor1: Ahmad Awad Ashare, A member of the Federal Somali Parliament
Explanation: A member of the Federal Somali Parliament, Ahmad Awad Ashare, accused Spain and South Korea of dumping toxic waste in Somali waters and engaging in massive illegal fishing operations with fake permits they allegedly got from individuals who do not have the authority to issue fishing permission. The MP also said they are destroying the ecosystem by tampering with coral reefs where fish breed. He called upon Somali people to "defend themselves against this problem."

A Yemeni fishing boat was stolen from the port at Mukalla in the middle of the night. The chairman of the Fishermen Cooperative Union (FCC) suspects it is now being used by pirates. Piracy has become a daily problem for Yemeni fishermen. Fishermen have become worried and are scared of going far from shore to fish. Pirates have taken over Yemeni fishing boats in the Gulf of Aden and the Indian Ocean, used fishers as human shields while carrying out attacks, and used Yemeni dhows as weapons storage facilities. Additiolly, Yemeni fishermen have been attacked by intertiol coalition forces as collateral damage in the fight against piracy or because they were mistaken for Somali pirates. Since the beginning of 2009, the Hadramawt District has lost about $150,000 (currency not given) because Yemeni fishermen are too afraid to sail into pirate infested fishing grounds. Many out-of-work fishermen have opted to stay at home and rely on government stipends.

Location: 75 miles off the coast of Hadramawt
Explanation: Approximately 75 miles off the coast of Yemen, an Indian val vessel approached a Yemeni fishing vessel thinking they were Somali pirates. The Indians forced all fishers to hand over their weapons and jump into the sea. Though the fishers said they did not have any weapons, they were forced to tread water for two hours before they could climb back on board. All of their fish spoiled. Yemeni fishermen are frequently caught in the middle of the battle between pirates and foreign powers trying to combat piracy.

The Russian vy detained 10 pirates that had been closing in on an Iranian-flagged fishing trawler near the Yemeni Island of Socotra. They were in possession of rifles, grede-launchers, and illegal rcotics.
Pirates hijacked a Yemeni fishing vessel in the Gulf of Aden. 7 of the fishermen maged to escape and return to shore in a small boat. 12 fishermen were still on the seized vessel.
Fishermen in a small fishing boat were run over and crushed by a foreign fishing vessel illegally fishing in Somalia's coastal waters because there was no enforcement mechanism to stop them.

Location: Gulf of Aden between Mukalla (Yemen) and Alula
Actor1: Yemeni fishing boat, Monfiq; South Korean Naval Ship as part of the multinational anti-piracy mission
Explanation: A South Korean naval vessel in the region as part of the international mission against piracy rescued five Yemeni fishermen on their boat, Monfiq, hijacked by Somali pirates to use as a mother ship in the Gulf of Aden

Actor1: Puntland Minister of Fishing and Ports
Explanation: The Puntland minister of fishing and ports accused the French navy of allowing French fishing boats to have access to Puntland waters without informing the regional administration. The minister said the French must stop bringing these fishing boats to the coast and be involved only in the anti-piracy mission

Actor1: Fishermen on the Yemeni fishing boat Mahrom; British naval warship
Explanation: Somali pirates hijacked a Yemeni fishing vessel, the Mahrom, that was sailing from Mukalla to Somalia to sell oil and fish. A British val warship rescued the Yemeni vessel and sent the Somalis back to Somalia.

Actor1: Muhammad Qanyare Afrah, a Somali member of Parliament
Explanation: A Somali member of parliament accused foreign warships stationed in along the Somali coast to combat piracy of stealing hundreds of tons of fish, lobsters, and other marine resources from the Somali waters on a daily basis. He says the foreign ships are only pretending to combat pirates. He also accuses them of dumping toxic waste in Somali waters

Location: Somali territorial waters, likely in Puntland
Explanation: Somali pirates hijacked a Yemeni fishing vessel, and the 5 member crew. There was reported resistance by the fishers, but no injuries or casualties were reported.

The French navy allegedly stopped and questions three fishing boats off the coast of Alula in Bari. The foreign warship was in the region as a part of the foreign joint efforts to combat piracy in the region. For an unclear reason, the French forces opened fire on the fishermen and killed two (one Somali and one Yemeni) and injured 4 men.

Actor2: Al Hilal, a Yemeni fishing boat
Explanation: Somali pirates hijacked a Yemeni fishing vessel, the Al Hilal, near Hafun. It was taken over by at least 14 Somali gunmen.

Actor1: Somali naval forces commander, Admiral Farah Ahmad Umar
Actor2: Foreign warships in Somali waters; Foreign fishing boats
Explanation: The Somali naval forces commander accused foreign warships that claim to be in Somali waters combating piracy or looting Somali marine resources and protecting their own foreign fishing vessels. The commander called upon foreign ships deployed in Somali waters to withdraw as soon as possible

Actor1: Somali fishing boat; Portuguese and Spanish navy
Explanation: The Spanish and Portuguese navies thwarted an attempted pirate attack of a Somali fishing vessel. The 5 pirates involved were arrested.
Somali pirates hijacked the Yemeni fishing vessel Almahmoud 2, with a crew of 15, in the Gulf of Aden.

Actor2: Foreign fishing boat, crew of Bengali and Indian fishers; Village of Eyl
Explanation: Somali pirates hijacked a foreign vessel on the high seas (flag of vessel not listed, but crew was from India and Bangladesh) and brought it back to the village of Eyl. Upon realizing the crew was from India and Bangladesh, the pirates decided to use the hijacked vessel to attack other ships, and use the hostages to help them, instead of wait for a ransom from the foreign government. The residents of Eyl decided they "would not let [the pirates] harm these poor people," so they prevented the boat from leaving and called Puntland authorities for help, forcing the pirates to free the fishers. Residents of the village told the media they were tired of the pirates dominating their town.

Location: Off the coast of southern Somalia, near Kismayo
Explanation: Somali pirates hijacked the Indian flagged dhow, Nafeya, in southern Somali territorial waters. The crew had 13 fishers and was attacked by approximately 9 pirates.

Actor1: Commander of the Somali Navy, Admiral Farah Qare
Actor2: Foreign military forces in Somali waters
Explanation: The commander of the Somali navy told foreign warships in the region to combat piracy that they have failed to address the problem.

A Yemeni fisherman was killed when Somali pirates tried to seize a Yemeni vessel with 9 crewmen off the coast of Baargaal. There is no information on what happened to the other 8 fishers or the Yemeni vessel

Three fishermen were kidpped by a gang of pirates that beat them up and tossed them into the Gulf of Aden. They were found alive on a Yemeni beach 3 days later. This story has caused most Yemeni fishermen to be too afraid to travel more than 20 miles off shore. Yemeni fishermen say their business has been cut in half. They also feel their lives are threatened by intertiol forces patrolling the waters.

Location: 151 nm East of Pemba Island, TZ
Explanation: Eleven Somali pirates hijacked a Kenyan-flagged Spanish-owned fishing vessel called the Sakoba in Kenyan waters between Kenya and the Seychelles. The vessel was intended to be used as a mothership. The vessel and crew (16 men) were released on July 24, 2010.

Somali pirates hijacked a Kenyan fishing vessel in Kenyan waters near the village of Kiunga. A fisher was able to alert the Kenyan security forces, who intervened while the vessel was en route to Somalia and arrested the 11 Somali pirates.

Somali pirates attacked an Iranian dhow with a crew of 13 men on it. The men were tied up for two days while the pirates collected all of the property, fuel, and food from the vessel. The pirates abandoned the vessel, but destroyed the telecommunication device, so the boat was floating in the Gulf without food or fuel for four days before it was rescued by a Spanish frigate.

Actor1: Al-Shabab Governor for Lower Shabelle; Fishermen in Lower Shabelle
Explanation: The Al Shabaab Lower Shabelle Governor, Sheikh Muhammed Adballah, issued a warning to warships in Lower Shabelle waters that a special unit of fighters has been mobilized to to fire against foreign warplanes and ships that harass local fishermen. He accused the warships of paralyzing Somalia's fishing sectors and causing panic among coastal residents. The warships are in the region to combat Somali piracy

Actor1: Students and Administrators from Omar Samatar secondary school
Actor2: Foreign fishers in Somali waters; Foreign warships in Somali waters
Explanation: Hundreds of aggravated students and administrators at the Omar Samatar Secondary School held a demonstration against IUU fishing and dumping toxins in Somali waters. They also denounced the foreign warships patrolling Somali waters, saying Somalia did not need them.

The US Navy allegedly raided the town of Hobyo, taking four people with three boats from the town. The Navy forced fishermen to suspend their operations while they were in town, creating a panic. The navy is in the region to combat piracy. Foreign navy warships have been accused of paralyzing the fishing service on the coast. The Violence Score for this event isa 2 with no arrests recorded because the arrests do not relate to fisheries conflict. The focus of this event from the perspective of this database is the halt in fishing operations.

Actor1: Hizbul Islam, a Somali Islamist political faction
Explanation: The Somali Islamist political faction Hizbul Islam accused the internationally backed effort to combat piracy in Somali waters of having a huge impact on the local fisheries sector by protecting trawlers illegally fishing in Somali waters. They claim the UN plan was to fight against the Somali fishermen and increase the political pressure in Somalia.

Actor2: Russian Navy warship, RFN Marshal Shaposhnikov
Explanation: Yemeni fishermen in 7 fishing boats near Qusay'ir were fired at by a Russian helicopter and forced to board the Russian vy destroyer RFN Marshal Shaposhnikov where the fishers claim the crew robbed them of their boats, money, IDs, GPS units, and clothes. Some of the boats were burnt. The fishers were then put on a single boat and sent back. The Russian vy shot at their boat as they sailed away. The Russian vy is in the region to combat Somali piracy. Yemeni fishermen claim harassment from foreign armadas has become a major issue for them.

A crew of 17 Yemeni fishermen was stopped by members of an Indian vy warship, who boarded their vessel and beat 11 and forced two men to jump into shark infested waters. Foreign warships are in the region to combat Somali piracy. Yemeni fishermen have said abuse by foreign vies has become a major issue for them.

Actor2: Foreign naval armadas; Governor of Hadramaut Province
Explanation: Fed up with abuse and harassment by foreign val armadas in the region to battle Somali piracy, Yemeni fishermen held a sit-in demonstration in Mukalla. They claim this abuse has costs them about YER 30 million in damage from searches and seizures. They say the forces in the region are often as dangerous as the pirates themselves. Fishermen agreed to suspend their sit-in if the governor of Hadramaut province promised to bring their problem to Sa'a. The fishers demand the countries of vies that have destroyed their boats pay them restitution.
AMISOM (African Union's Mission in Somalia) troops have been accused of killing a Banaadir fisher and taking away his fishing equipment and boat.

Actor1: Regional Administration of Galmudug; Galmudug troops to combat foreign fishers
Explanation: Authorities in the regional administration of Galmudug announced they have prepared hundreds of troops to combat foreign ships illegally fishing in Galmudug waters. The administration noted that dozens of foreign ships are stationed just off the Galmudug coastline and they have harassed, tortured, killed, and arrested innocent fishermen.
The Royal Thai Navy sent 350 personnel to join the international mission to combat Somali piracy and armed robbery. The main duty of the Thai operation is to protect Thai cargo and fishing vessels.

Actor1: Puntland Ministry of Ports forces; Puntland Ministry of Ports
Explanation: The Puntland Ministry of Ports forces seized a boat illegally fishing along its coast. The 8 member crew was made up of 7 Somalis and 1 foreign fisher.

Location: 14 nm off the port of Balhaf, Shabow province
Explanation: Somali pirates seized a Yemeni fishing boat 14 nm off the port of Balhaf

Location: 7 km off of Abd al Kuri Island
Explanation: Six Somali pirates hijacked a Yemeni fishing vessel near Abd al Kuri Island. One of the hostages said they had to work for the pirates for 25 days. Local fishermen in Hadhramaut are afraid to go fishing and are keeping close to the coastline, even though their catches are very poor there.

Gunmen from a fleet of foreign fishing vessels shot at, and seriously injured, two Somali fishermen. Local officials say there are not enough resources to combat foreign vessels illegally fishing in the region.

Actor1: Somali men hired to protect the vessel
Actor2: Thai fishermen; British and US Naval forces
Explanation: 3 Somali men hired to protect a Thai fishing trawler decided to seize the ship, Sirichai Nava 12, and hold its crew of 26 for ransom. The British and US Navies worked together to intercept the vessel, free the hostages, and arrest the hijackers. One of the Thai fishers experienced a minor flesh wound.

Actor1: Rival fishing firm owner, Abdullah Ali Mohammed of Dubai; Armed Henchmen of boat firm rival Abdullah Ali Mohammed
Actor2: Indian fishers; Fishers' employer, Pramod Ranjan
Explanation: Eight Indian fishermen were held hostage on their Dubai-owned fishing vessel by henchmen employed by a rival fishing firm owned by Abdullah Ali Mohammed. The employer of the hostages said there was a disagreement regarding profit sharing with Mohammed. The event ended when the hostages' employer agreed to hand over one of his fishing vessels in exchange for the crew.

Actor1: Somali pirates called the National Volunteer Coast Guard (ex-members of the militia Jubba Valley Alliance)
Actor2: Fishers on the Taiwanese vessel Chung Yi 218
Explanation: Three Taiwanese fishing vessels, Chung Yi 218, Cheng Ching Feng, and Hsin Lien Feng 36, were hijacked at separate times in the waters of Lower Juba by Somali pirates that were ex-members of the Juba Valley Alliance militia.The vessels were held near Kismayo. The boats had paid application fees for fishing rights to local authorities (the local warlord), but their hijackers alleged the vessels were fishing illegally. Negotiations to free the vessels (48 hostages total) were a challenge because Taiwan does not have diplomatic relations with Somalia. The Chung Yi 218 was released January 27, 2006.

Actor1: Somali pirates called the National Volunteer Coast Guard (ex-members of the militia Jubba Valley Alliance)
Actor2: Fishers on the Taiwanese vessel Cheng Ching Feng
Explanation: Three Taiwanese fishing vessels, Chung Yi 218, Cheng Ching Feng, and Hsin Lien Feng 36, were hijacked at separate times in the waters of Lower Juba by Somali pirates that were ex-members of the Juba Valley Alliance militia.The vessels were held near Kismayo. The boats had paid application fees for fishing rights to local authorities (the local warlord), but their hijackers alleged the vessels were fishing illegally. Negotiations to free the vessels (48 hostages total)  were a challenge because Taiwan does not have diplomatic relations with Somalia. The Cheng Ching Feng was released January 30, 2006.

Actor1: Somali pirates called the National Volunteer Coast Guard (ex-members of the militia Jubba Valley Alliance)
Actor2: Fishers on the Taiwanese vessel Hsin Lien Feng 36
Explanation: Three Taiwanese fishing vessels, Chung Yi 218, Cheng Ching Feng, and Hsin Lien Feng 36, were hijacked at separate times in the waters of Lower Juba by Somali pirates that were ex-members of the Juba Valley Alliance militia.The vessels were held near Kismayo. The boats had paid application fees for fishing rights to local authorities (the local warlord), but their hijackers alleged the vessels were fishing illegally. Negotiations to free the vessels (48 hostages total)  were a challenge because Taiwan does not have diplomatic relations with Somalia. The Hsin Lien Feng 36 was released January 30, 2006.

Kenyan fishers were tricked into fishing in Somali waters by their employer (they were told they would be in Kenyan waters). While in Somali waters, the were approached by two speed boats that fired at them. The fishers had weapons on board and managed to evade the attack.

Location: Somali territorial waters, likely near Kismayo
Explanation: The Taiwanese vessel Feng Rong 16 was hijacked by Somali pirates in November 2005 and held with three other Taiwanese fishing vessels, the Ching Yi 218, the Cheng Ching Feng, and the Hsin Lien Feng 36. The Feng Rong 16 was released January 30th, 2006

Actor1: Fishers on Indian fishing dhow, MV Safina al-Birsarat; US Navy
Explanation: 10 Somali pirates hijacked an Indian dhow, the MV Safina al-Birsarat, and held its crew of 16 hostage while they used the vessel as a mothership to hijack other ships. The dhow's crew later told the courts they were regularly beaten and denied food. Following a failed hijacking of a merchant vessel, a US Navy warship seized the pirates and freed the crew. The crew was sent to Kenya to be tried for piracy, where they claimed they were fishermen abducted by the Navy during a fishing outing. The court found them guilty. The Somali transitional government does not have the capacity to deal with the piracy problem and has asked other nations to help. In their court case, the pirates claimed they were just fishermen who didn't understand why they had been abducted by the Navy. The Kenyan court convicted them of piracy.

Location: Somali waters between El-Maan and Kismayo
Explanation: The Indian fishing dhow Bhakti Sagaar, with a crew of 21, was hijacked by armed Somali pirates in Somali waters. The pirates approached on 2 speedboats. The crew and vessel were released on March 29, 2006. The Somali government is asking for international assistance to control the piracy issue.

Location: Somali territorial waters, 25 nm off coast
Actor1: US Naval Warships USS Cape St George and USS Gonzalez
Explanation: Two US Naval Warships exchanged gunfire with a pirate vessel, a fishing boat, off the coast of Somalia. The pirates, who claim to be militiamen, but provide no militia name, were on a mission to protect the Somali waters from illegal fishing and damage to their marine resources. There are mixed reports on which vessel opened fire first. One of the Somali men was killed, five were injured. The twelve surviving Somalis were arrested and brought to Kenyan court.

Actor1: South Korean tuna trawler, Dong Won No. 628
Explanation: Eight Somali pirates hijacked the South Korean tuna fishing trawler the Dong Won No. 628 off the coast of Somalia. The took the crew of 25 hostage. The pirates have said they are defending their resources from illegal fishing and acting as the coast guard. The vessel had obtained a fishing permit from the transitional government. Vessel and crew were released July 30, 2006 after ransom was paid.

Location: Yemeni Island East of Somalia (Coder assumes Socotra Island)
Explanation: The Somaliland Coast Guard (Yemeni authorities called them pirates) arrested 50 Yemeni fishermen and seized their 8 boats off the coast of a Yemeni Island east of Somalia.
