Explanation: Three SSDF officials complained about driftnet fishing off Somali coast by foreign trawlers, saying foreign boats were entering Somali waters illegally, as all fishing licenses issued by former dictator Siad Barre had expired (foreignfisher, unlicensed). They warned foreign trawlers "to stay clear of Somali territorial waters" until they had obtained permits from the new authorities, stating that "long-range artillery and armed speed boats" would be deployed along the coast to stop the slaughter of marine life (ecochange). They claimed foreign trawlers were taking advantage of turmoil/clan warfare (weakgov) to empty the Somali coast of fish (fishpop).

Actor1: Two Taiwanese boats: Cherng of Yue Sa No 3 and Chian Yuein No 232
Explanation: The Somali Salvation Democratic Front (SSDF) seized two Taiwanese boats and will put their captains and crew on trial for illegal fishing off the Somali coast (illegal: unlicensed, foreignfisher, groundlim), as well as for using internationally banned fishing practices (increased efficiency) seriously damaging the environment (ecochange). The SSDF said foreign boats were taking advantage of the situation of clan warfare (weakgov) to exploit and steal Somali maritime resources (fishpop). SSDF officials said they can provide fishing licenses to those who request them.

3,400 Somalis seized a fishing vessel (Gob Wein) in which to escape war and starvation at home (FoodInsecurity, Poverty, WeakGov). They sailed across the Gulf of Aden to Yemen, where they were initially refused landing.
"A Somali faction took military action, leading to the killing of a Taiwanese crew in Somali waters." Article deals with illegal fishing increasing off the coast of Somalia.

Gen. Ali Ismail, an SSDF militia commander, issued an ultimatum to the foreign fishermen off of SSDF-claimed territory in Northeasten Somalia: Leave Somali waters in one week or prepare for attacks by armed SSDF speedboats. Article discusses how foreign fishers are plundering Somali fish stocks (fishpop) and that they are using prohibited equipement (drift nets -- increfficiency)

Actor1: 23 Filipinos on Taiwanese fishing vessel (Ching Chi Nsing-Ang 3)
Explanation: Somalia militia abducted crew of 23 Filipinos on a Taiwanese fishing vessel, demanding a ransom, described as a fine for violating Somalia's territorial waters (foreignfisher, groundslim) and compensation incurred by the militia during the Filipinos' month in captivity (thus did not mark 'Crime'). Nine of the fishermen reportedly released on March 10, 1994, but news reports cited uncertainty about the status of the remaining hostages. c4017 appears to refer to the same hijacking: "At the beginning of February, a Taiwanese fishing vessel was captured by a mini-flotilla of Sambuks, was armed with machineguns, bazookas and light guns, and sent out to capture half a dozen small Pakistani and Yemeni fishing vessels (where the ransom was reportedly set at between $ 30,000 and $ 40,000 each)." c4017 also states that the owner of the Taiwanese vessel recuperated it after paying $420,000 in early March.

Location: Crew mutinied in Somali waters after ship departed Djibouti and forced it to head towards Bosaso. Ultimately detained in Bosaso.
Actor1: Crew of Italian-registered fishing vessel Airone; Meridionale Pesca - a firm that owns both the Airone and the Giuseppe (another fishing vessel - see IssueNote)
Actor2: Mejertein pirates; Yussuf Mohamed 'Bari Bari', SSDF's former representative in Italy and signatory of an October 1993 fishing agreement with Meridional Pesca
Explanation: Some Somali crew members on the Airone mutinied on August 5 after the vessel sailed from Djibouti, and forced it towards Bosaso, where it was taken over by two pirate leaders. The Mejertein maritime inspectors presented a fine of $270,000 to the shipowner because the Airone crew had been using too-fine meshed fishing nets (increfficiency). They also demanded a fine of $4 million from the shipowners because another vessel (the Giuseppe) was accused of illegal fishing off of Bosaso (unlicensed, foreignfisher, groundslim). The Airone was released on 9/10/1994 after ship owner paid $250,000. The shipowners are accused of contravening the fishery agreement signed at end-1993 between Meridional Pesca of Bari (south-east Italy), and the former representative in Italy of Somali Salvation Democratic Front (SSDF), Yussuf 'Bari Bari', It does not appear that the fine of $4 million was paid, because the shipowners claimed to have had sold the Giuseppe to Kenyan owners in October 1993, so it was no longer covered by the fishing agreement.

Actor2: Osman Mahmoud sub-clan headed by 'Boqor' (king) Abdulahi Musse 'King Kong'
Explanation: During the SSDF congress, a resolution tabled by the Omar Mahmoud sub-clan denounced "certain individuals known for having misappropriated funds from fishery activities", an implicit attack on Yussuf Bari Bari, Abdulahi Boqor Muse King Kong, and Mohamed Abshir. The accusation is in reference to a fisheries agreement signed at end-1993 between Meridional Pesca of Bari (south-east Italy), and the former representative in Italy of SSDF Yussuf 'Bari Bari', which provided that 10 percent of catches  would be made available to the Somali partners, who had also been provided with hundreds of thousands of dollars.

Location: Jilib-Merca Beach, 26 km South of Mogadishu
Actor1: 11 Pakistani and 1 Palestinian fishers; Leaders of two other Somali factions: SSA and a rival USC/SNA faction
Actor2: United Somali Congress/Somali National Alliance (USC/SNA) faction
Explanation: 12 foreign fishermen were "arrested" by gunmen in March while fishing off Jilib-Merca beach. The captives said they used to fish off the Somali coast and sell their catch in Mombasa. In August, the captives went on a hunger strike (c4030). Their captors threatened to kill those who went on the hunger strike and two were badly beaten to frighten the others into ending the strike. Lack of food and poor sanitary conditions in the building, coupled with the high room temperatures, also caused serious skin diseases such as scabies among the captives. (Thus, counted 12 injuries). Critics of Aidid (leaders of SSA faction and a rival USC/CNA faction) demanded the captives immediate release and termed their detention "an act of terrorism". A civilian court organized by Aideed's faction sentenced them to 5 years imprisonment for illegal fishing, but on 10/5/1995 Aideed ordered their release. However, it was not clear whether/when they were released (c4036; hence, ongoing). Some sources said Aideed's faction asked for a ransom from the Pakistani government (c4030; hence, Crime).

Location: between Ras Alula and Ras Scenage, off Cape Guardafui
Actor1: "western companies, probably Italian" which are discharging toxic waste in Somali waters
Actor2: Boqor (king) Abdullahi Musse (aka King Kong), tribal chief of the Osman Mahmoud subclan of Mejertein in northeastern Somalia and Yassin Farah Artan, deputy chairman of SSDF; SHIFCO
Explanation: Musse (aka King Kong), tribal chief of the Osman Mahmoud subclan of Mejertein in north eastern Somalia launched an international SOS on October 11 to combat "western companies, probably Italian" which are discharging toxic wastes at sea off the Somali coastline. His letter gives the exact coordinates of the site of pollution (between Ras Alula and Ras Scenage, off Cape Guardafui), and the data justifying the international SOS call can only have been supplied by SHIFCO, which recently signed a fisheries agreement with Musse covering the waters off of Bosaso which also provided that SHIFCO would assist the local Somali authorities in maritime surveillance in the zone. SOS co-signed by SSDF's Yassin Farah Artan. Musse indicates that the toxic discharges had been seen by local eyewitnesses and seemed to be carried out in concert with officials of certain Somali factions.

Actor1: Militiamen loyal to General Mohamed Said Hirsi Morgan; New Zealand-owned Morris Fishing Company and Russian fishing group
Actor2: Militiamen loyal to Mohamed Haji Aden
Explanation:
Militia loyal to General Mohamed Said Hirsi Morgan attacked militia loyal to the current Somali VP. Mohamed Haji Aden. Aden tried to stop fishing by foreign companies (e.g., Morris Company - New Zealand - and a Russian company) from fishing in the Lower Juba river in Kismayo. Morgan had brokered the agreements allowing the companies to fish there. There are political undertones as the General recently defected to the United Somali Congress. Four died, seven wounded. Clan elders in Mogadishu stepped in and restored security. (c4040: However, Morgan's spokesman in Mogadishu said only one police_x000D__x000D_
 officer at an Islamic Sharia court in Kismayo had been injured.)

Location: Red Sea (not specified in Yemen, but ship manager was in Yemen during negotiations)
Actor1: 37 people on board including 5-7 foreigners from Tanzania, Italy, and Portugal
Explanation: 4 Somali gunmen employed aboard the SHIFCO fishing vessel Farax Omar took control of the ship while it was fishing in the Red Sea after they learned their contracts had been termited. They forced the captain to steer it to the coastal town of El-Der (Somalia) where it was held at Igo (Ego) Beach. They demanded a ransom and the ship was released on Sept. 27, 1996. c4045 states: "the gunmen are demanding for a huge amount of money for the release of what they called the illegal fishing vessel"

Actor1: foreign crew of the "21-October-3", including two Italians and one Portuguese; Somali crew of "21-October-3" (unclear how many were Somali)
Actor2: Somali gunmen (disgruntled ex-crew of Farax Omar)
Explanation: The same Somali gunmen who hijacked the Farax omar (e3166) hijacked the 27-October-3, a fishing vessel which is also owned by SHIFCO. They demanded "contract termination severance" (backpay), in other words a ransom, in order to release the two vessels (Crime). Illegalfishing was not mentioned for this ship. Ship released following payment of a ransom on Nov. 3, 1996.

Location: Gara Ad, some 600 km north of Mogadishu
Actor1: crew from Italy, Poland, Romania, Kenya, and Senegal; crew from Somalia
Actor2: Local Somalis in four armed speedboats
Explanation: Local Somalis in 4 armed speedboats forced a fishing boat to the coastal village of Gara Ad. 34 foreign nationals on the vessel are being held and charged with illegal fishing. Article mentions foreign vessels taking advantage of fish stocks (fishpop) and the lack of central authority to prevent this (weakgov). It also mentions that the fines and judgements imposed by local militiamen and faction leaders are often arbitrary. The crew was released 4 months later after paying a fine of $200,000.

Location: Ego beach, about 340 km North of Mogadishu
Actor1: about a dozen somalis on two small fishing boats
Actor2: four foreign fishing ships flying the flags of greece, south korea, pakistan and italy
Explanation: About a dozen somalis on two small fishing boats equipped with assault rifles attacked four foreign ships that had looted fishing nets with lobsters valued at more than USD 12,000. The foreign vessels used heavy machine guns which forced the local fishermen to retreat to the mainland, while the big ships went out of sight. The head of a local fishing company accused the foreign vessels of looting (crime) and fishing illegally in Somali waters (illegalfishing, foreignfisher, groundslim). He said this is the third time in two months that nets were looted by big vessels, and local fishermen are now working with heavy machine guns on their boats. He threatened they will destroy every foreign fishing vessel they see in their area of operation.

Location: Adale district beach, 100 east of Mogadishu
Explanation: A net looting business involving foreign vessels occurred at Adale district beach. Mohamud alasow kurdi, deputy district commissioner, said the foreign vessels have looted all the nets laid by the local fishermen (crime, foreignfisher). Two of the local fishermen were wounded while fighting with these foreign fishing vessels at the district, about 100 km east of mogadishu.

Location: Elman beach, 30 km North of Mogadishu
Actor1: two small Somali fishing boats; Ahmed Hassan Wata
Actor2: motorized foreign fishing vessels illegally plying Somali waters
Explanation: Motorized foreign fishing vessels opened fired on two small boats on Thursday, but the boats managed to return safely to Elman beach, 30 kilometers (18 miles) north of the Somali capital. Motive for attacks is unclear, but head of a Mogdishu-based fishing company Ahmed Hassan Wata says "Fishing trawlers, mainly from Italy and other Far East Asian countries, currently fishing illegally in Somali waters, have been making life difficult for Somali fishermen in the last three months." Article mentions lack of government to protect coast line (weakgov). In response to the attack, Wata's company will send anti-aircraft launching boats to the beaches to protect the fishermen. The Elman Port Authority will also send high range heavy machine-guns used by the former Somali coast guard to the area to contain the threat.

Actor2: Abdi Warsame Jama, security officer for the Gara'ad Fishing Corporation in northeastern Somalia, and SSDF militiamen
Explanation: Somali gunmen with the SSDF arrested the captain and 27 crewmembers of a Taiwanese vessel. An Islamic court charged them with fishing illegally in Somali waters and the prosecutor called for a double amputation of the captain (cutting of one hand and one feet). The court  charged a fine totaling about USD1 million divided between the ship's owner ($800,000), the captain ($40,000) and $10,000 from each of the crew. The court said failure to pay the fines would lead to the confiscation of all the cargo aboard the ship, including 50 tonnes of shark fins and other seafood products. It also said the ship itself would be auctioned off. The captain of the ship claimed he had obtained a fishing license from a company called Afmat but the judge dismissed the claim (the prosecutor said the permit was fake). The court is also pursuing charges against the Shu Hen Bin fishing company, said to own the trawler, over three other trawlers that escaped Somali arrest at the same time.

Actor1: Crew included Italians, Kenyans, Polish, Senegalese, Romanians; Crew included 4 Somalis
Explanation:
A large vessel with more than 34 foreigners aboard was caught fishing off Mudug region of central Somalia. The chief of Gara-Ad coastal village said the ship was captured by four well-armed speed boats _x000D__x000D_
off the seashore before the occupants were taken to Jarriban town of Mudug region for_x000D__x000D_
questioning

Location: Habo village, 70 km East of Bossaso

Explanation: SSDF militia seized 2 Cyprus-flagged vessels off the coast of Habo village and detained 35 foreigners, charging the two ships with illegal fishing. The Syrian government claimed ownership of the two ships. The captains were charged dlrs 500,000 each. The two ships may not actually have been fishing ships, but included in FDE because SSDF charged them with illegal fishing. They are variously referred to as Cyprus-flagged (c4060), owned by the Syrian government (c4060, c4063), and Bulgarian (c6047, c4070). See notes for a link to outside source which appears to refer to the ships in question: "In January 1998, militiamen in northeast Somalia captured two foreign ships, a Bulgarian freighter towed by a Syrian vessel.  Elders and businessmen in Bosasso, northeast Somalia, helped negotiate release of the crews on 13 February 1998 in exchange for $110,000."

Actor2: Pirates (also referred to as Somali militiamen)
Explanation: Two French yachtsmen sailing around the Horn of Africa were kidnapped off the Somali coast and their vessel seized by militiamen. The SSDF accused them of illegally entering Somali's territorial waters. SSDF said they had endangered local fisherman by coming too close to fishing water controlled by SSDF.

Actor1: Italian-owned, Kenyan-registered fishing vessel, the MV Bahari Hindi; A second Italian-ownder, Kenyan registered fishing vessel which escaped capture
Explanation: Somali gunmen in four armed boats attacked two fishing vessels belonging to Kenyan-based Italian fishing company. The leader of the gunmen said some of his men were wounded, as the vessels were equipped with heavy machine guns. One vessel escaped, taking away a Somali gunman who had boarded it. The other vessel (the mv Bahari Hindi) was detained at Eyl and its crew of 33 were charged and convicted of destroying marine life (ecochange), "stealing Somali marine products, illegally entering Somali waters, possessing weapons and destroying property." They were fined $500,000.  The gunmen said their action against the foreign fishing vessels had the support of the regional government in Puntland. Regional authorities also captured the entire catch of the vessel and threatened to sell the trawler and jail the crew if the fine was not paid in time. The foreign fishers did not receive legal representation in their trial. Articles mention lack of central government to protect coast (weakgov). The vessel was freed in February 1999 after paying more than $230,000 in fines for fishing illegally (c4103). According to c4103, local fishermen claimed the vessel was arrested after it had destroyed their nets and fishing gear and shot at their boats. The crew of the MV Bahari were robbed of their possessions prior to their release (c4107). In c4264, a crewmember said the crew was beaten while in detention, one until unconciousness.

13 Somali gunmen arrested 4 Ukrainian fishers for illegal fishing. Two were collecting sea shells on a beach near Alula and the other two were on their vessel nearby. The gunmen said they would not ask for a ransom but would hand the fishers over to the Puntland authorities and request reimbursement for the costs of detaining the 4 men. The 4 claimed to have sailed from Sri Lanka but had no food onboard their ship and had only a high frequency radio with the capability of contacting other vessels in Aden. In January 1999, the Ukrainians went on hunger strike demanding to be released; they said they were tourists and had not been fishing illegally (c4100). Reports say they are being held in appalling conditions.

Location: Baydh district east coast (Should be Maydh?)
Explanation: The Somaliland coast guard impounded two foreign (Djibouti-registered) ships for illegally fishing in Somaliland waters, off Baydh district east coast. Crew members were held at the Maydh police station

40 Yemeni fishermen were detained by Somaliland authorities for illegally fishing near the region's Red Sea Coast. Six fishing vessels (three large trawlers and three smaller vessels) were confiscated from the Yemeni fishers. 4 Somalians were also arrested for collaborating with the foreigners. The crew was fined a total of USD 21,000 by a court in Berbera.

Actor1: Taiwanese fishing vessel with 31 foreigners onboard
Explanation: Over 30 gunmen in 4 armed speedboats detained a Taiwanese fishing vessel with 31 foreigners onboard. The vessel is charged with illegally fishing in Somali waters. One of the speedboats that attacked the vessel and six of its gunmen are still missing.
Gunmen detained a Ukrainian fishing vessel with 18 Ukrainians and 1 Somali national onboard. The vessel is charged with illegally fishing in Somali EEZ waters.

The Somaliland marine security surveillance teams have seized five fishing boats and arrested at least 60 Yemenis for fishing in Somaliland's territorial waters, off the coast of Seylac, illegally. They were later granted amnesty by the president and released (c4138).

Location: somewhere between Eldher (Galgudud region) and Kismayo
Actor2: local fishermen; North Mogadishu faction Marine Resources Unit
Explanation: Two speedboats operating with the North Mogadishu faction Marine Resources Unit attempted to arrest foreign fishing vessels fishing illegally in Somali waters. The foreign vessels responded by firing heavy machine guns (and water canons) at the speedboats. Article states they are accused of shooting at local fisherman and authorities, so Domestic Fishers included as an actor. Article mentions lack of central governance (weakgov) and that the foreign vessels use illegal fishing methods such as poison (increased efficiency, illegal fishing: gear). The head of the Marine Resources Unit warns: "Although we do not intend to kill anybody extra-judicially, we will send boats built with anti-aircraft weapons to destroy the fishing vessels."

Actor1: a ship belonging to a Swiss firm
Actor2: Hard Group (foreign private security contractor hired by Puntland authorities); Puntland regional authorities who hired Hard Group
Explanation: Puntland officials confirmed that a ship belonging to a Swiss firm, which illegally caught over 400,000 metric tonnes of fish, was captured. Six workers from the ship were also arrested. A foreign firm, the Hard Group, which the Puntland administration has contracted to carry out surveillance of Puntland's territorial waters, has captured foreign owned boats and ships illegally fishing the Puntland waters.

Actor1: Pakistani and Indian nationals; Puntland Attorney General
Explanation: Three local officials are illegally holding more than 30 Pakistani and Indian nationals and their two vessels in northeastern Somalia for allegedly illegally fishing in Red Sea waters controlled by Puntland. A Puntland official said the foreigners have been held for more than 6 weeks without the knowledge of the Attorney General, and their wellbeing is unknown. The 3 officials have been ordered to appear in a Bossasso court on May 13 to answer charges of abuse of power in connection with the fishermen's detention, but they refused the order.

Location: Bargal, 30 km east of Port of Bosaso
Actor2: Somali gunmen; Hart Group (private security contractor)
Explanation: Somali gunmen seized a French-registered cargo ship near the town of Bargal, 30 km east of Bosaso. They accused the crew of illegal fishing (unlicensed, foreignfisher, groundslim). They also stole items including generators, food, fuel and the crew's personal belongings (Crime). Puntland authorities got involved to negotiate the ship and crew's release and may have made military threats against the gunmen. The ship was handed over to Hart Group on July 13th, and supposedly released. However, according to c4155, reliable sources claimed that as of July 20 the ship was still being detained by Hart Group in Puntland waters and may be suspected of dumping nuclear waste. However, c4189 (published in September 2001) states that the crew indeed was released by the Hart Group a few days after they were originally detained.

Actor2: crew members of a fleet of fishing vessels accused of fishing illegally
Explanation: Fighting broke out in Eyl, Nugaal District, between local fishermen and crew members of a fleet of fishing vessels accused of fishing illegally in the district. All kinds of weapons were used in the fighting and the heavily-armed crew of the fishing vessels wounded two local fishermen. They also inflicted serious damages on some of the vessels owned by the residents of the area and tore up more than 50 fishing nets.

A group of Somali fishermen and armed men on board a Korean fishing vessel exchanged fire on the coast of Marka [southern Somalia]. Although no casualties were reported the vessel is believed to have destroyed 50 fishing nets owned by the Somali fishermen.

Actor2: does not specify (just says the ship was seized and then tried by a local court)
Explanation: A Korean ship was recently seized at Jariiban coastal town, Puntland, and a local court imposed a fine of 300,000 US dollars on the ship for illegally bringing in fishing equipment and exporting charcoal out of Somalia.

Actor2: Does not specify who rescued the fishers from their sinking ship and then scheduled them in court
Explanation: At least 30 Pakistani sailors are expected to appear in court in the northeastern Somali coastal town of Eyl. The sailors are accused of fishing illegally in Somalia's northeastern coastal waters. The suspects were earlier rescued from a ship which sunk in the northeastern coastal region of Somalia. The court is expected to impose a heavy fine on the suspects for illegally fishing in Somali waters.

151 fishermen from Yemen and Djibouti are being charged with illegally fishing in Somaliland territorial waters. 15 small boats used by the fishermen were detained for a few days after their capture by armed boats belonging to Somaliland. The prosecution is calling for confiscation of all marine products from the fishermen and to punish the 151 fishermen.

Actor2: Armed militiamen from the Somali National Front
Explanation: 20 Kenyans and 8 Russians who were onboard two trawlers, MV Horizon I & MV Horizon II (Also called MV Gorizont I & II), were held by Somali gunmen for 6 months. The gunmen demanded $80,000 from one of the ships owners. President Hassan spoke out against the kidnapping and publicly apologized. The circumstances of their capture are somewhat unclear (some articles state they were detained in February 2001, but it seems they were actually detained in November 2000 and their captivity became known in February). Some reports indicate they were held because of illegal fishing in Somali waters while others state they were held because of a business deal turned sour. They were all released as of May 7, 2001, though one of the ships appears to have returned to Kenya in mid-March.

The head of the Hart Group, [an international security firm] contracted by  untland to provide coast guard services, has told reporters that his company is pulling out of Puntland because the Puntland government has violated the contract it had signed with the company. The move follows the decision by Abdullah Yusuf [Puntland president] to sign new agreements with Taiwanese and Chinese [fishing] companies as well as the man who is holding the old fishing fleet of Somalia, Eng Munye. Hart Group is a British firm with its headquarters in America and has 600 soldiers fully equipped to guard the Puntland coastal waters.

Actor1: Kenyan, Senegalese, Italians, one Spaniard, one Romanian fisher
Actor2: Militiamen, believed to be loyal to warlord Jamal Farah
Explanation: Militiamen armed with machine-guns (believed to be loyal to warlord Jamal Farah) stormed MV Bahari Kenya, a Kenyan-registered fishing vessel used by an Italian firm. The militiamen claimed it was fishing without a license and detained the 32 crew members. An Italian diplomat said a license had been obtained from Puntland's fishing ministry.  In late August, Somali elders fined the vessel $700,000 for fishing illegally in Somalia's territorial waters. The crew was released traumatized but unharmed in Augsut 2001, following payment of the fine.

At least six people were killed and eight were wounded in a clash over a fishing area in the northeastern Somali coastal village of Kulul. Two refrigerated vehicles, a truck with a mounted gun and a fishing boat were destroyed in the fighting. Fighting erupted when one of the groups tried to fish in an area reserved for the other in violation of an earlier agreement.

A fishing ban was introduced after massive deaths on the Lamu shores to allow the scientists to find out the cause (which was determined to be algal bloom). Fishing activities on the islands of Pate, Kiwayuu, Ndau, Kiunga ya Pili and Lamu came to a standstill raising fear of serious shortage of fish in the Lamu archipelago. At least 20,000 people living in the area are likely to face fish shortage when the current fish stocks stored in cold storage are exhausted between one and two days. The fishing ban was removed on Feb. 13 after the toxic bloom subsided.

Location: El-Wargadhi village, 160 kilometers northeast of Mogadishu
Explanation: Local fishermen exchanged heavy gunfire with what they believe to be foreign vessels fishing illegally in Somali waters. The five large vessels do not fly any flags and didn't have any markings on their bows. No one was hurt during the fighting, but stray rounds from the vessels have killed several sheep grazing along beach. The fighting has forced all ten local boats to come ashore and stay out of business since Wednesday. A local fisherman said in the past they were able to scare away the vessels by shooting at them, but now they have bigger guns. Fishermen on more than 100 small boats up and down the coast are now afraid to go to sea, seriously hampering the industry.

Actor1: 6 Pakistanis and 4 Somalis on the MV Mogadishu
Explanation: Six Pakistanis and four Somalis were detained for entering Kenya illegally aboard the fishing vessel the MV Mogadishu. They then remained in detention and were questioned in connection with November 2002 attacks against Israelis in Mombasa. The article states there is no evidence linking them to the attack and an anonymous businessman stated: "They are illiterate and poor people who have nothing to do with the Mombasa attacks on Israeli interests. â€¦ The case is too dangerous and security officials can, therefore, suspect anybody found near those places." The captain of the MV Mogadishu was likely also among several Pakistani's held in 2000 for illegally fishing in Somali waters.

Actor2: local fishers; Over a dozen gunmen in speedboats
Explanation: In August 2002, a North Korean-registered, Greek-owned cargo ship was detained by Somali gunmen in Puntland. According to Farah Dohajog, the minister of commerce and industries of Puntland, the vessel was abducted after it crushed a small fishing boat owned by local men and killed four men, destroying 21 fishing nets in the process (Crime). Dohajog also said the ship was accused of dumping toxic waste (EcoChange). The gunmen were negotiating for a ransom to release the crew, but on February 8, 2003, the vessel escaped captivity and sailed away.

Actor2: Gunmen employed by Raas (a Somali fishing firm)
Explanation: Somali gunmen employed by the Raas fishing company detained an Egyptian fishing vessel and its crew of 24, claiming that the owners of the ship owed the company compensation for breaking their contract with the firm. 23 of the crew members were released on Feb. 23, but the vessel along with 1 crew member remained in detention as of that date.

MV Beira 3 was captured by the ship's Somali agent in March 2003 after disagreements over fishing royalties and security provided to her previously. The agent is reported to have demanded $985,000 (Sh78 million) ransom to release the crew members. 10 Kenyan crew members were released in February 2004, while the remaining 11 Koreans and Indonesians were released in July 2004. c4239 also mentions the kidnapping of MV Beira 3's sister ship, the MV Beira 9, in mid-2003; however, according to c4240, the manager and a crew member of the MV Beira 9 refuted that it was ever captured.

Three Egyptian fishing vessels have been seized by Somaliland forces after they continued to fish in Somaliland waters following the expiration of a fishing license issued to them by Somaliland authorities. A local court in Berbera charged the vessels a $1,000 fine. However, the Somaliland Minister of Fisheries expressed dismay, stating that only the high court has the authority to issue such fines, and that the amount charged was too small. The Berbera court claimed it has authority because the vessels were fishing in areas under their jurisdiction.

Actor2: regional security forces of Galmudug state
Explanation: Regional security forces of Galmudug State seize foreign fishermen in Hobyo town of Mudug region.
Regional security forces of Puntland administration conduct security operation, seize foreign boats involved in illegal fishing in northeastern coastal town of Eyl.

Actor2: crew of UAE-owner oil tanker Aris 13; Puntland marine police force
Explanation: A group of up more than two dozen Somalis captured a UAE-owned oil tanker with a crew of 8 Sri Lankans between 18-30 km off the coast of Somalia near Alula. The Somalis claimed to be fishermen who were targeting the ship in order to call attention and protest the presence of illegal foreign fishers in Puntland waters. An elder said the group of fishermen included former pirates and had been sailing in search of a foreign ship to hijack for two days. He said foreign fishermen had destroyed their livelihood (Poverty) and deprived them of proper fishing. A coast guard official said that at least 15 pirates along with some fishermen had hijacked the ship, stating "I can confirm to you that Somali pirates were involved" (Crime). He also said the pirates were demanding a ransom (what they call "compensation" for a rise in illegal fishing) and an end to foreign illegal fishing. One of the alleged pirates said in a statement: "We have decided, as local fishermen , to resist illegal fishing . We have taken arms to defend ourselves, and we will continue." According to c7293, c7294, c7300, c7309 the hijackers claimed to capture the ship after they saw it dumping toxic waste into the sea (Ecochange). Locals interviewed reported that foreign trawlers sometimes attack, ram their boats, open fire or destroy the gear of local fishers (Marginalization). John Steed from Oceans Beyond Piracy also states that food in the region is scarce because of the drought, and fishermen out of work due to the presence of foreign trawlers are pushed towards piracy (FoodInsecurity). c7275 states that foreign trawlers have a technological advantage, dropping big nets that harm ecosystems while local fisherman may have just a fishing rod (IncEfficienty). According to Oceans Beyond Piracy, some trawlers also use aggressive methods to stake out claims to fisheries, stealing and destroying rivals' equipment and even hiring armed guards to fend off competition (OppScales). Fake licenses granted to foreign fishers, lenient enforcement of regulation by local authorities, and licenses granted to too many foreign trawlers are also cited as factors allowing illegal fishing to flourish and fostering anger among locals (WeakGov). "With the seas empty of fish because of toxic waste dumping and illegal fishing by foreign trawlers, many Somalis say they have no choice but to turn to piracy to survive" (FishStocks). Explanation: The fisheries dispute event involves the capture of three Peruvian fishing boats in the Santa Clara Island Marine Reserve in southwestern Ecuador. The boats were apprehended by the Navy and other entities during a surveillance operation in the protected area. The presence of nets and the crew members engaged in fishing were discovered, which is illegal in the reserve. The operation resulted in the retention of 400 pounds of common fish and mollusks that serve as food for marine fauna and birds. The Ministry of Environment emphasizes the importance of patrolling the area to protect the ecosystem and biodiversity. Invading a marine reserve is considered an environmental crime and is punishable by imprisonment. The Santa Clara Island Marine Reserve is located south of Puna Island and is known for its abundant marine fauna and bird populations.

Location: High Seas west of the Galapagos Islands
Explanation: This article describes a fisheries dispute event between the United States Coast Guard and Chinese squid trawlers in the eastern Pacific Ocean near the Ecuadorian Galapagos Islands. The Coast Guard was on a mission to search for signs of illegal fishing when several Chinese vessels fled and one veered toward the Coast Guard schooner, causing an evasive maneuver to avoid collision. The incident is a violation of international maritime protocol and has raised concerns about China's fishing fleet, which has seen a significant increase in the number of vessels fishing in the South Pacific and unsustainable squid fishing.
Summary: The US Coast Guard was on a mission to search for signs of illegal fishing when several Chinese vessels fled and one veered toward the Coast Guard schooner.
